title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream that flows through our rohe and holds deep cultural importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream is named for the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks and provided medicinal resources. Students will connect with the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden, whose tears are said to feed our awa, understanding the interconnectedness of water systems.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data (pH, nitrate levels, turbidity) from three specific sites along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific equipment.
Compare scientific water quality data with mātauranga Māori indicators of stream health (presence of tuna, kōura, and specific riparian plants).
Develop a kaitiakitanga action plan proposing one evidence-based intervention to improve the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

activities.title: Whakatau: Karakia ki te Awa & Introducing Hinepūkohurangi
activities.description: The lesson begins at the stream's edge with a karakia (led by a kaumātua or kaiako) to acknowledge the awa and ask for safe passage in our work. Students will hear the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi and discuss what her story teaches us about the value and origin of water.

activities.title: He Rārangi Inenga: The Science of Water Testing
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students work in small groups to collect samples from a designated site (e.g., near the college, upstream near a reserve, downstream near a culvert). They will measure and record quantitative data: pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature.

activities.title: Ngā Tohu Ora: Mātauranga Māori Health Indicators
activities.description: Using identification sheets developed with local kaumātua, students search for and record qualitative mātauranga Māori indicators of health. This includes looking for kōura (freshwater crayfish), observing the types of plants on the bank (like harakeke), and checking for signs of tuna (eels).

activities.title: Te Whakataurite: Data Synthesis and Korero
activities.description: Back in the lab, groups compile their quantitative and qualitative data onto a shared class map of the awa. They analyze the data to identify trends and correlations (e.g., do higher nitrate levels correlate with an absence of kōura?). They discuss their findings in the context of the pūrākau and modern land use.

activities.title: Te Mahere Kaitiaki: Developing an Action Plan
activities.description: Each group proposes a specific, evidence-based action to enhance the stream's mauri. Proposals must integrate their scientific findings and mātauranga Māori, e.g., 'Planting kotukutuku and harakeke on eroded banks to reduce turbidity and provide habitat, as our tūpuna did.'

resources: Local Kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu (e.g., Aunty Hine Smith) to share pūrākau and mātauranga.
LaMotte Water Monitoring Kits or similar for student water testing.
NIWA's online 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources.
Identification guides for native freshwater species and riparian plants, co-created with the local marae.

tasks: Submit a completed field data sheet with scientific and cultural observations.
Create a comparative graph (e.g., bar chart of nitrate levels vs. sites).
Write a one-page 'Kaitiakitanga Proposal' justifying their chosen action with evidence from both data sets.

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science (AO: Plan an investigation; Gather and interpret data); Participating and contributing (AO: Explore various aspects of an issue and make decisions about possible actions).
Living World: Ecology (AO: Investigate the interdependence of living things in an ecosystem).
